--

How I used AI agents to rebuild an npm diff library’s core in C and WebAssembly: a 300,000-word book compared in 147 ms, entirely in the browser.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Comparing two versions of a text is one of the oldest problems in computing. It is also a central problem in the humanities. A scholar collating two witnesses of a manuscript, an editor checking a proofread chapter against the draft, a translator comparing revisions of the same passage: all of them are running a diff.
On a paragraph, any library works. On a book, most of them start to hurt. The classic dynamic-programming approach to word-level comparison needs an N×M matrix, which at 30,000 tokens per side means roughly 3.5 GiB of memory for a single comparison. Even the good JavaScript implementations, built on smarter algorithms, take hundreds of milliseconds on novel-sized inputs, and their memory behavior is often unpredictable. In a browser tab, that is a real problem.
So I built arena-diff, an open-source npm library that does word-level diffing with a C engine compiled to WebAssembly. On a book of about 300,000 words per side, it produces a complete word-level diff in 147 ms, using about 55 MiB of working memory, a size it can tell you before it starts. On typical editorial revisions it is 2.5–3× faster than the fastest comparable token-level library I could find on npm.
The speed is only part of the story, though. The library got fast through an iterative optimization loop run with AI coding agents and refereed by a benchmark suite. I think that loop applies to many other libraries, and it is the main reason I am writing this post.
Why texts, why the humanities
Most diff tools are built for source code. Lines are the natural unit, and the output is a patch. Prose is different: the natural unit is the word, revisions are dense and scattered rather than block-shaped, and the people doing the comparing are often not developers at all.
Digital humanities has a whole discipline devoted to this, called collation, and its own standard for recording the results: TEI P5, the XML vocabulary used by digital critical editions. arena-diff ships a toTeiDiff() export that turns a raw diff into scholarly markup:
The quick <subst><del>brown</del><add>red</add></subst> fox<add> </add><add>jumps</add>.A substitution becomes <subst>, an insertion <add>, a deletion <del>: the exact elements an edition would use to record a variant. The idea is that the distance between "I computed a diff" and "I have markup I can put in an edition" should be one function call.
The performance requirements of this audience are real, too. A critical edition does not compare two paragraphs; it compares witnesses, meaning full texts, sometimes many of them, pairwise. At book scale, a 3× speedup separates an interactive tool from a batch job.
A different set of trade-offs
Most npm diff libraries are pure JavaScript; a few wrap a Rust core. arena-diff takes a third path: a small C engine, about one file, compiled to bare WebAssembly with Clang and wasm-ld. No Emscripten and no runtime. The .wasm binary is inlined into the package, so npm install works as-is in Node, browsers, and bundlers.
The pipeline splits the work between the two languages according to what each is actually good at:

• JavaScript tokenizes. Intl.Segmenter splits the text into words, spaces, and punctuation using real Unicode rules, something the JS runtime already does well and C does not.

• Every unique token becomes a 32-bit integer. The C engine never sees a string; it compares int32s, one instruction each.

• The IDs cross into WASM with zero copying. They are written straight into WebAssembly linear memory through a typed-array view, with no JSON and no per-token marshalling.

• The C engine does the algorithmic work: a linear-space Myers diff (the same family of algorithm Git uses) plus a layer of heuristics on top.

• Results come back as counts first, details later. compare() returns keep/insert/delete counts immediately; the full operation list is a lazy getter, so you only pay for object hydration if you actually read it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The library takes its name from the memory strategy: a single pre-allocated bump arena that scales as O(N+M) with the input, about 90–95 bytes per token in total. There is no malloc and no garbage collector at work. estimateArenaBytes(n, m) tells you the exact working-set size before you commit, which matters in a browser tab or an edge worker with a memory budget.
The algorithm layer follows the same philosophy of bounded behavior. Pure Myers is optimal but degrades badly when two texts differ a lot. So the engine first strips the common prefix and suffix, then finds anchor points: 16-token windows that appear in both texts, located with a rolling hash and filtered into a consistent chain with a longest-increasing-subsequence pass. Myers then runs only inside the small gaps between anchors. When a gap is huge and has no anchors, guardrails kick in: a linear-time guided diff that produces a valid (if not strictly minimal) edit script instead of letting the runtime blow up. Correctness is enforced by round-trip tests: applying the diff to text A must reproduce text B exactly, every time.
Where the AI agents come in
The interesting part, to me, is not the diff algorithm. Myers published that in 1986.
Everything described above is known engineering. Arena allocation is old. Rolling-hash anchoring is old. If npm is nonetheless full of diff libraries that allocate gigabytes or fall over on book-sized inputs, it is not because nobody knows these techniques. Applying them takes a lot of unglamorous work: writing the C, wiring the WASM boundary, building the benchmark harness, then grinding through round after round of profiling, hypothesis, and measurement. For most maintainers of most libraries, that work never clears the bar of being worth the time, and the wins below the API stay unclaimed.
AI coding agents change that calculation. They do not have ideas that humans lack; they make the grind cheap enough to actually do.
The workflow on arena-diff looked like this. First, the ground truth: a benchmark suite (research/ in the repo) with synthetic scenarios at fixed seeds (light revision, heavy rewrite, completely dissimilar texts, at 15k and 30k words), plus a memory profiler and a round-trip correctness check. Every proposed change had to survive all of it. Then the loop: the agent profiles, proposes an optimization, implements it, and the benchmarks deliver the verdict: keep or revert.
The changelog of that loop is a series of small, measured wins:

• Flattening the Myers V-array indexing to avoid modulo operations: ~1.6× on the core diff routine.

• Hash anchors plus LIS chaining, the big one: it is why heavily revised 30k-word texts diff in ~100 ms instead of several hundred.

• Trimming matched prefixes and suffixes inside every Myers recursion, not just globally: less work per gap.

• Packing operation codes as int8 instead of int32: 75% fewer bytes for the result stream.

• Making the ops list lazy on the JS side: counting-only calls became nearly free.
None of these is a headline on its own. Together they separate “another diff library” from the fastest one in every scenario I tested.
A word on the division of labor, because this is where posts about AI-assisted development tend to drift into fiction. The agent did not design the architecture: the hybrid JS/C split, the arena, and the decision to target TEI were mine. Its proposals were not uniformly good either. Some optimizations benchmarked flat or regressed and were reverted; the benchmark suite existed precisely because a plausible-looking patch to a diff engine is worth nothing until it is measured. What the agent contributed was throughput. It made each iteration of the loop cheap, so the loop could run many more times than my patience would otherwise allow. The judgment stayed human, and the verification stayed mechanical.
The numbers
All benchmarks are synthetic (random prose, fixed seed, reproducible with npm run research:compare in the repo) and measured end-to-end, tokenization included. The comparison baseline is myers-core-diff, the second-fastest token-level library in my tests.
On 30k-word texts with ~8% revision, a realistic proofread-vs-draft scenario, arena-diff finishes in 35 ms against 109 ms (3.1×). On a heavy 50% rewrite at the same size, where pure Myers suffers most, the gap widens to 101 ms vs. 652 ms (6.5×). The advantage holds at book scale:
Words per side arena-diff myers-core Speedup
---------------------------------------------------------
~40k (novella) 20 ms 43 ms 2.1x
~80k (novel) 38 ms 94 ms 2.5x
~120k (long novel) 56 ms 154 ms 2.7x
~300k (omnibus) 147 ms 452 ms 3.1xPress enter or click to view image in full size

Memory is where the design shows most clearly. At ~15k tokens, arena-diff’s working set is 2.7 MiB, exact and computed in advance. One popular WASM-based alternative ranged from 242 MiB to 3.7 GiB on the same workloads, growing unpredictably. For a tool meant to run in a browser next to a scholar’s edition, that predictability matters as much as raw speed.
Two caveats. These are synthetic corpora, not your corpus; the repo includes the full benchmark suite, so you can check on your own texts before believing me. And libraries that skip materializing the full edit script (like diff-sequences) use less memory still; they are just much slower at scale and give you less to work with.
Limitations
This is a v0.1 with a deliberate scope. It is word-level only: no line or character granularity yet, so it is not a jsdiff replacement for source code. On enormous anchor-free gaps the edit script is valid but not guaranteed minimal (correctness is enforced by round-trip, not by shortest edit distance). It is ESM-only and needs Intl.Segmenter (Node ≥ 18, modern browsers). Line-diff, char-diff, and unified-patch output are on the roadmap.
The loop, generalized
If you maintain a library where performance matters, or depend on one, the transferable lesson is not “rewrite it in C”. It is the shape of the loop:

• Build the referee first. Reproducible benchmarks and a hard correctness check, before any optimization. Without them, an AI agent is a generator of plausible nonsense.

• Keep the architecture human. Agents are good at exploring within a design; choosing the design is still your job.

• Let the agent grind. Profile, propose, implement, measure, revert or keep. The cycle that used to be too expensive to run twenty times no longer is.
A lot of performance sits below the APIs of the libraries we all use, guarded less by difficulty than by tedium. That guard is weakening.

arena-diff is MIT-licensed:
npm install arena-diffCode, benchmarks, and an algorithmic deep-dive are on GitHub. If you work with large texts, editions, translations, or publishing pipelines, I would like to hear how it behaves on your corpus.
